content:
8 min read
Just now

--

The Agile Manifesto put people over processes and tools. Twenty-four years later we finally took it literally: our project management is software we wrote ourselves, the standup answers itself, and the AI sits on the crew instead of beside it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The first line of the Agile Manifesto, written by seventeen people at a ski lodge in Utah in 2001, values “individuals and interactions over processes and tools” [1]. For twenty-four years the industry recited that line and then went out and bought the tools anyway. Jira. Asana. Monday. Linear. We put the tool in the center and arranged the humans around it.
This year we stopped. We didn’t switch tools. We deleted the category. Our project management now runs on software we wrote ourselves, shaped to our teams instead of the other way around, and it has changed how we work more than any framework, ceremony, or certification ever did.
I want to show you two pieces of it, because the abstract version (“build your own AI-native tooling”) means nothing until you see what it looks like on a Tuesday morning.
The reset
We treated this as a structural decision, not a software purchase. Running our own project management is now baked into how the company is put together, the same way you’d decide your legal structure or your hiring model. Not a pilot. Not a skunkworks experiment that reports back in a quarter. The default.
The logic is simple once you say it plainly. Every external tool is built for the average of ten thousand companies, and no company is that average. So you spend the first month bending your team to fit the tool’s idea of a workflow, and you spend every month after that maintaining the gap between what the tool assumes and what you actually do. For decades that was the rational trade, because building your own was a year of engineering nobody could spare.
It isn’t anymore. Building the fitted version, the one that matches our teams, our development tools, our cadence, our own definition of done, is now a matter of days. So we built it. And then we kept building it, because the second advantage turned out to be bigger than the first: it changes as fast as we do.
The spaceship
Our task board is a spaceship.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Not as a metaphor. Literally: a screen that looks like the bridge and interior of a ship, with systems, stations, and a crew. Every task is a job somewhere on the vessel. You can see at a glance which systems are lit, which are dark, where the ship is under strain, and who is on deck.
The reason it’s a ship and not a list of tickets is that a ship has a crew, and our crew now includes the AI. Some stations are worked by people. Some are worked by the AI. Many are worked by both, with the machine taking the first pass and a person taking the helm when judgment is needed. The interface makes that visible. You can watch the AI pick up a task, carry it, and hand it back at the exact point where a human has to decide something. Where the ship needs a person, the ship shows it.
Underneath the pixels it is still an ordinary agile task system. Requests come in, work gets pulled, things move across states. What is new is the AI’s place in it. Instead of a bot bolted onto the side, filing tickets like a very fast intern, the AI is a character on the crew, with a place on the bridge and a personality in the room. The old task board asked “what needs doing?” Ours also asks “who, or what, is doing it, and where does the machine stop and the person begin?”
That single change, giving the AI a seat on the crew instead of a plugin slot, reorganized far more than the screen. It reorganized the team.
None of this is fixed, either. The ship is the shape our work has today. A company is a dynamic thing, a team even more so, and what they need changes over time. Because the software is ours and the AI rebuilds it with us, the interface adapts along. Tomorrow it might be a sailing boat. Next year, a building we manage floor by floor. The system follows the company, instead of the company following the tool.
The standup that answers itself
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here is the ritual we all inherited. Every morning the team stands in a circle and each person answers three questions: what did you do yesterday, what will you do today, and is anything blocking you. Scrum codified those three questions, and for years they were the entire script of the daily standup [2]. Scrum’s own authors eventually removed them, because teams were reciting status instead of planning. Most teams kept reciting anyway.
We still ask the three questions. We just stopped asking the humans.
Now the questions go to the AI, and they are phrased from the worker’s point of view. What are my tasks? Where are my blockers? What did I actually do yesterday? The AI has been watching the work happen the whole time. It sees the commits, the moved tasks, the messages, the stalled reviews. It doesn’t need me to reconstruct yesterday from memory and present a tidy summary. It already knows, more accurately than I do, and it usually knows where I’m stuck before I’ve admitted it to myself.
So the standup inverts. Instead of humans reporting status upward into a tool, the tool reports reality outward to the humans. The tracking, the part that was always everyone’s least favorite chore and the reason half the tickets were forever out of date, is simply done. By the AI. Continuously. The meeting, on the mornings we still hold one, is finally free for the conversation that the status updates were always crowding out.
What breaks when the tool bends
You can’t do any of this and keep Jira. That’s the uncomfortable part for the whole tooling industry.
Satya Nadella said the quiet thing out loud when he predicted that business applications, most of which are “a CRUD database with a bunch of business logic,” would collapse in the agent era as the logic moves into the AI [3]. Project management software is the purest example he could have chosen. Strip a task tracker down and it is a database of tickets with rules about how they move. Those rules used to live in the tool, which is exactly why you bent your process to fit them. Now the rules live in an AI that watches how your team actually works, and the database is just where it keeps its notes.
When Andrej Karpathy described the new normal of software work as “orchestrating agents” rather than writing code yourself [4], he was talking about developers. But the same inversion lands one layer up. If agents are doing the work, the system that tracks the work has to treat agents as first-class members of the team, not as a webhook that files a ticket. None of the incumbent tools were built for that. So we aren’t waiting for them to catch up. We’re building it ourselves, and it has been one of the best decisions we’ve made.
The crew
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The thing I didn’t expect is how much this changed the people, not just the machines.
When the tracking is automatic and the AI is a visible member of the crew, the standup stops being a performance and the board stops being a debt you are forever paying down. People spend the reclaimed attention on the actual work and on the decisions the AI hands back to them. The AI isn’t a replacement looking over anyone’s shoulder. It’s a crewmate that takes the repetitive work and hands the interesting decisions back to the people best placed to make them, and the team is happier for it. The org chart flattens a little, because “who is doing what” gets answered by the ship rather than by a manager collecting updates. And the line between human work and machine work stops being an anxious abstraction. It becomes something you can point at on the bridge.
The acceleration
Here is what it all adds up to. The chores are gone: the reporting, the tracking, the task management, the coordination overhead that used to eat a slice of every single day. The AI does that now. It also does a large share of the tasks themselves. Our job has shifted from executing the work to orchestrating it.
Orchestrators move faster than executors. With the first pass of most work already done and nobody’s morning lost to updating tickets, delivery stops improving by percentages and starts multiplying. What used to take a quarter now ships in weeks, and what took weeks ships in days.
The surprise is that the speed came with quality attached. The AI works inside our system, so it follows our norms, our testing, our quality bars, our process, on every task, every time. A rushed human skips steps; the AI never does. We ship faster and we ship a better product, and those two stopped being a trade-off the day the chores disappeared.
Agile was right in 2001. Individuals and interactions over processes and tools. We just needed the tools to get cheap enough, and smart enough, to finally get out of the way. Ours did. It turns out the fastest route to putting people first was to stop renting our process from someone else and build a ship of our own.
References

• Kent Beck et al., “Manifesto for Agile Software Development” (Snowbird, Utah, February 2001).

• Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland, The Scrum Guide; the three Daily Scrum questions were prescribed in earlier editions and dropped in the November 2020 revision.

• “‘Hey, why do I need Excel?’: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella foresees a disruptive Agentic AI era that could ‘aggressively’ collapse SaaS apps,” Windows Central (December 2024).

• “Vibe Coding Is Passe. Karpathy Has a New Name for the Future of Software,” The New Stack (2026).
